payload.text:
app where you input your name/names and get a deep research report on it. use this prompt: Context:
Geography: [Region, if known, or "Unknown"].
Known Facts: [e.g., family legends, social status, migration history].
Research Protocol:
Etymology: Analyze the linguistic root. Is it patronymic, professional, topographic, or a nickname? Check for roots in relevant language groups.
Geographic Distribution: Identify historical "hubs" of this surname prior to the 20th century using census data and regional records.
Social Analysis: Determine which social classes or groups typically held this name in the specified region.
Variability: Check for phonetic distortions and common spelling variations in historical registers.
Output: A structured report including a list of specific archives (e.g., National Archives, church records) and document types for further verification.  have a text box for name. and another for any additional optional info. generate illustrations of and related to the name

**Conceptualizing Report Features**

I'm currently focused on the initial input methods for this React application. I'm prioritizing the name input field, which will be the core of the report generation. I'm also considering the integration of optional geography and known facts/additional information fields to refine the research reports. The goal is to collect the necessary data to feed the structured prompt for Gemini.


**Refining Project Scope**

I've established the key components and features for the report generation application. It's React 18+ with TypeScript, styled with Tailwind CSS, and utilizes the Gemini API. The reports will cover Etymology, Geographic Distribution, Social Analysis, and Variability, with structured outputs and suggested archives, all complemented by generated illustrations. I've also defined the necessary file structure and UI elements.


**Defining Report Output**

I'm now zeroing in on the report's structure: Markdown with added structure. I'm also planning the export functions—print and copy. I must adhere to the provided prompt template, incorporating the exact specifications for the research report. For image generation, I'll leverage `ai.models.generateContent` with the image model, specifically using `gemini-2.5-flash-image`. The text model will be `gemini-3-pro-preview`.


**Refining Implementation Details**

I'm now fully immersed in the coding phase. I've decided to implement the report component with a card-based layout and I'm playing with a typography with serif headings. The color scheme will be parchment, deep blues, and gold accents. I'm also addressing the image loading speed concern—I'll use a placeholder or show the text report first. The exact user prompt is integrated precisely as content within the API calls now.


**Launching API Integration**

I'm now integrating the Gemini API service, focusing on the `geminiService.ts` file. I'm excited to connect the front-end to the model. I'll make sure to get the request structure right, and establish the request's core components.

entries.description: Primary entry point served in the browser.
Primary entry point of the application.
Service for interacting with the Gemini API for text and image generation.
Form for inputting name research details.
Detailed display component for the generated research report.
Main application component with layout and state management.

payload.text: in addition to the header image. have images under each of the information tabs . also, print button downloads a document with all information and photos. properly layed out

**Considering Image Integration**

I'm now focused on enhancing the "Onomastica Research Lab" application. My current goal is to integrate images under each information tab—Etymology, Geographic Distribution, Social Analysis, and Variability—within the application. I am thinking about how these images can best enrich the existing data.


**Rethinking Print Functionality**

I'm now pivoting to the print function's behavior. Instead of the basic print dialog, I plan to create a highly styled, print-ready view. The user will then be able to "Save as PDF" through their browser's built-in functionality. This approach minimizes dependencies and provides a user-friendly "downloadable document" experience. I'm also considering the time constraints of generating multiple images.


**Enhancing Image Generation**

I'm now thinking about how to generate those images. I've updated the plan: I'll focus on generating four images, one for each section, by updating the Gemini service. I plan to use `gemini-2` for this. The UI updates are in progress as well, and I'll ensure the print function incorporates all images. My new goal is to create a print stylesheet to make the "Save as PDF" process effortless. I am considering providing a downloadable HTML file as well.


**Optimizing Image Retrieval**

I'm now streamlining the image generation process. I've realized `gemini-2.5-flash-image` is the default, so I'll optimize image retrieval with this. I'll update `generateResearchReport` to handle this logic or implement a dedicated function for the section images. The UI will feature archival-style illustrations under each research section, and I'll adjust the layout accordingly. I'm prioritizing deferred fetching to optimize the main report's initial display. Finally, I'm working on a "Download Dossier" function that creates a standalone HTML document or enhances the print view with an archival stylesheet.


**Focusing On Implementation**

I'm now going with a multi-image approach alongside a refined "download" experience, not just print. The new plan is to ensure that the print CSS produces a great PDF. I'll start with updating `types.ts` and `geminiService.ts`. I also settled on image prompts: for etymology, I'll go with linguistic roots and ancient scrolls; for geography, antique maps and migration routes; social images will include heraldry, and for variability, I'll use handwriting samples. It's time to get to work.

payload.text: make text in text box black. it currently isnt visible when typed out. also downnload research isnt working

**Investigating Visuals & Functionality**

I'm currently focused on two key areas: improving text visibility in the input fields and ensuring the "Download Formal Research Document" button functions correctly. I've located `ResearchForm.tsx`, where the input fields have a `bg-white/80` style, which might be the root cause of the color issue.


**Clarifying Print Functionality**

I've been examining the "Download Formal Research Document" button. The user's feedback ("download research isn't working") has me looking at the `window.print()` call within. I'm thinking the issue might be that it doesn't clearly indicate to the user that it opens a print dialog where they can "Save as PDF". I need to verify if the function itself is triggering the print process; otherwise, I may need to debug further, but not add a heavy dependency. Further, I am now digging into `ReportDisplay.tsx`, which may also have implications.


**Evaluating Download Behavior**

I've shifted focus to the "Download" button behavior. I'm exploring the implications of calling `window.print()` directly. I realize that users might anticipate a direct file download rather than a print dialog. The existing logic is in `ReportDisplay.tsx`, which is rendered within `main` in `App.tsx`. I noticed that `textReport` in `App.tsx` is being generated, and `setReport` called, but `generateSectionIllustration` is then called multiple times, which might cause the user to see placeholders.


**Refining Print Presentation**

I'm now revisiting the overall print presentation within the app. I'm focusing on `ReportDisplay`, specifically the print-specific styles `print:p-0` and `print:max-w-none`, to ensure proper layout. I'm also thinking through the impact of the `App.tsx`'s background style (`bg-stone-50`) and its `main` container, which might clip content during printing. I think I will use the `window.print()` call, since it preserves CSS well. I'm aiming to improve the print styles for a "properly laid out" document.


**Adjusting Text Visibility**

I'm now zeroing in on the text color within the input fields in `ResearchForm.tsx`. I'll enhance the visibility by adding `text-stone-900` to the `input` and `textarea` classes. As for the "Download" button, I'm sticking with `window.print()` for its simplicity. I will also make sure it's triggered by a user click. I'm going to look at adding some presentation enhancements to the "print view". I will need to verify its behavior is not blocked.


**Confirming Download Mechanics**

I'm now revisiting the "Download" button's function. The feedback is about a lack of download, so I'll prioritize verifying that the button click correctly triggers `window.print()`. I'll also add `text-black` to the input fields in `ResearchForm.tsx`. In `ReportDisplay.tsx`, I am going to enhance print styles, ensuring images are printed, page breaks are managed, and setting a white background for print. To make the output look more official, I will add a document header. I'm noting that the `tailwind` CSS is loading correctly, so I can target the text color with `text-stone-900` or `text-black`.


**Optimizing Print Presentation**

I'm now revisiting the "Download" button's function. The code in `ReportDisplay.tsx` already uses `window.print()`, so that's good. Now, I want to use a more "formal" layout for the print version. I'm going to apply the earlier fixes and verify the results.

entries.description: Make input text explicitly black/dark for visibility and ensure placeholders are legible.
Refined the print/download functionality and added robust print-specific layouts to ensure it looks like a high-quality historical document when saved as PDF.

payload.text: since generating/downloading doc is now working, take a full page screen shot of  the entire page and save it as image

**Re-Evaluating Download Strategy**

I'm revisiting the "Download Research" functionality. My initial approach was using `window.print()` for complex layouts with AI-generated images, but that's apparently not cutting it. It looks like a proper file download is the desired outcome. I'm exploring direct image or PDF generation now.


**Considering Screenshot Implementation**

I've been examining the user's latest request – they specifically want a full-page screenshot saved as an image. This clarifies the "download research" issue. It seems a previous suggestion to use `window.print()` was off-base. I'm now exploring the implementation of a full-page screenshot, recognizing the need for a library like `html2canvas` or `dom-to-image` for a reliable client-side solution. I'll need to figure out how to import it.


**Focusing On HTML Changes**

I'm now zeroing in on the UI updates. I've updated the import map in `index.html` to include `html2canvas`. Next, I'll integrate it within `ReportDisplay.tsx`. I'll also ensure that the black text fix in `ResearchForm.tsx` from the last step is preserved. I'll make the text color `text-black` to be safe, but I suspect the earlier, very dark color I used (`text-stone-900`) may have been responsible for the "text not visible" issue.


**Finalizing Implementation Details**

I'm now nailing down the specifics. The plan is locked in. The import map in `index.html` already has `html2canvas`. Next, I'll update `ReportDisplay.tsx` to call `html2canvas` on the `#research-report` element within its `handleDownload` function. Lastly, I'll verify the `ResearchForm.tsx` is using `text-black`. I've confirmed that `https://esm.sh/html2canvas` is the correct ESM package link for `html2canvas`.

entries.description: Add html2canvas to the import map for full-page screenshot functionality.
Ensure input text is explicitly black and background is solid white for maximum visibility.
Implement full-page screenshot capture using html2canvas to save the report as an image.
